comments:
Summary
The paper describes a framework for interactive construction of graphical causal models based on causal mechanisms encoded as structural equations. The authors extend Simon's causal ordering algorithm to handle under-constrained systems so that partial (incomplete) models can be visualized and iteratively refined. They describe implementation of these ideas in an interactive tool (ImaGeNie) built on SMILE/GeNie and discuss knowledge representation choices (mechanisms, manipulability/observability annotations, hierarchical organization) and the model-building workflow (selection, merging, manipulation, extraction of mechanisms). Several theorems are stated about the extended causal ordering; proofs are referred to the author’s thesis. An extended example (university budgeting fragments) illustrates the approach.

Strengths
- The paper is well-motivated: structure of causal models is crucial and model-building is a human-driven, iterative activity.
- It connects well-established theoretical foundations (structural equation models, Simon’s causal ordering) with practical needs for interactive modeling and tool support.
- The extension of causal ordering to under-constrained systems is a useful contribution: providing an informative graphical representation during incremental model construction helps users reason about unresolved causal directions and manipulation effects.
- The implementation (ImaGeNie) and integration with a real package (SMILE/GeNie) increases the practical relevance of the work.
- The paper situates itself among relevant prior work (KBMC, OOBNs, learning from data) and identifies complementarities.

Weaknesses / Limitations
- Evaluation is limited: there is no user study, controlled experiment, or quantitative assessment of how the tool or algorithm improves model-building efficiency, model quality, or user understanding. Claims about usefulness are plausible but largely anecdotal and illustrated with a toy/representative example rather than empirical evidence.
- Several formal results/theorems are presented but full proofs and detailed algorithmic descriptions (e.g., complexity, implementation choices) are deferred to the author’s thesis. This reduces the paper’s self-containedness and makes it harder to judge the technical rigor from the provided text alone.
- The paper would benefit from more detailed discussion of how reversible mechanisms are detected/represented in practice and how numerical parameters are handled when structure changes (CP T reversal, re-solving algebraic systems). The discussion notes these issues but leaves many open questions.
- Comparison to alternative approaches (KBMC, object-oriented or fragment-based model construction, and structure learning from data) is conceptual; an empirical or case-based comparison would strengthen the claims about where this approach offers real advantages.
- Some formatting/typographical issues in the provided text make parts harder to read (likely an artifact of the provided extract rather than the original paper). 

Suggestions for Improvement
- Include at least a small user study or qualitative evaluation showing how modelers interact with the tool, and/or examples demonstrating time-to-model or error reductions compared to a baseline.
- Provide either a fuller sketch of the extended causal ordering algorithm (pseudocode) and complexity analysis in the paper or an appendix, so readers can assess practical scalability.
- Clarify how reversible mechanisms are represented and how the system decides when to flip causality or when manipulation causes large structure changes; give concrete rules or heuristics used in the implementation.
- Elaborate on handling of numerical parameters after structural changes: when can CP Ts be re-used, when must they be re-elicited, and whether symbolic solving is practical for real-world submodels.

Overall Assessment and Recommendation
This paper presents a well-motivated, practical integration of causal mechanism representations, an extension to causal ordering for under-constrained models, and a working interactive tool. The work is a useful contribution to the body of research on model construction and tool support for causal/Bayesian modeling. The main weaknesses are limited empirical validation and deferral of detailed proofs/implementation specifics to external documents. I recommend acceptance as a contribution worth disseminating to the community, but I urge the authors to bolster evaluation and include more self-contained technical detail in a final version.

Recommendation score (1-10):
7
